pattern: Fixing a common 'floor' group size s and requiring every group to have size s or s+1 turns the problem into: find the largest s for which every value's count can be split into that few groups, since a bigger floor size always needs fewer groups when it is achievable.
hints: Every group holds copies of a single value, and the biggest group can be at most one larger than the smallest, so at any moment there are really only two allowed group sizes: some s and s+1.

For a fixed floor size s, a value that appears c times needs at least ceil(c / (s+1)) groups; that many groups only works if it is also possible to keep every group at least size s, i.e. that group count times s does not exceed c.

Try candidate floor sizes s starting from the smallest value's own count down to 1, and take the first (largest) s that is achievable for every distinct value; sum the minimum group counts for that s.

edge_inputs.description: two values with counts that split unevenly
three distinct values with mixed counts
every ball identical, one group suffices
one rare value forces small group size for everyone
counts that are prime relative to each other so no shared floor size larger than one works well
all distinct values, one ball each

stress.description: many distinct values with random skewed counts
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    num_distinct = max(1, min(n, rng.randint(1, 50)))
    values = rng.sample(range(1, 1000000000), num_distinct)
    balls = []
    remaining = n
    for idx, v in enumerate(values):
        left = num_distinct - idx
        take = 1 if left == 1 else rng.randint(1, max(1, remaining - (left - 1)))
        take = max(1, min(take, remaining - (left - 1)))
        balls.extend([v] * take)
        remaining -= take
    rng.shuffle(balls)
    return [balls]
